text.title: AARRR Dashboard and Product Examples
text.summary: Weekly dashboard template, SaaS/e-commerce/mobile app worked examples, and implementation checklist.

section.title: Weekly Dashboard Structure
section.p: The weekly AARRR dashboard has five sections, one per stage. Acquisition: visitors, sign-ups, CPA by channel, week-over-week delta. Activation: rate, TTV. Retention: cohort table with D1/D7/D30 for recent cohorts. Revenue: MRR, new MRR, churned MRR, ARPU. Referral: invites, conversions, K-factor.

example.note: Project management tool. Acquisition: 2,000 monthly sign-ups. Activation: 35% completed first project (target 50% — this is the priority fix before scaling acquisition). Retention: D30 at 18%. Revenue: 3% free-to-paid conversion. Referral: K-factor 0.05. Priority: activation is the bottleneck; fixing it from 35% to 50% would increase downstream revenue by 43% with zero additional acquisition spend.

Acquisition: 50,000 monthly visitors. Activation: 12% add-to-cart. Retention: 8% repeat purchase within 30 days. Revenue: 2.5% overall conversion rate. Referral: 1% share rate. Priority: retention (8% repeat purchase) — email re-engagement sequence and loyalty program are highest ROI next moves.

Acquisition: 5,000 downloads/month at $2.50 CAC. Activation: 40% created first workout. Retention: D1 55%, D7 30%, D30 15%. Revenue: 4% free-to-premium conversion, $8/month ARPU, $48 LTV. Referral: 5% invite rate, 15% referral conversion, K-factor 0.075. LTV:CAC ratio 19:1 — healthy economics, main lever is improving D7 retention to D30.

list.item: Define one key metric per stage before building any dashboard.
Set up tracking in GA4, Mixpanel, or Amplitude with one event per funnel step.
Validate activation event by correlating candidate actions with D30 retention in cohort data.
Create weekly dashboard with targets per stage and week-over-week deltas.
Review weekly; identify worst-performing stage; run one experiment to improve it.
